benign tumor: In the image, a benign tumor typically appears as a well-defined mass with smooth, regular borders.
The photo shows a clearly circumscribed lesion that displaces adjacent structures rather than invading them.
A benign mass often has a homogeneous appearance, with a uniform internal texture and signal intensity.
This scan displays a lesion with a distinct capsule or boundary that separates it from surrounding normal tissue.
The growth pattern visible in the photo is expansive, meaning it pushes nearby tissues aside as it grows slowly.
This is an image of a mass that is typically rounded or oval in shape, consistent with a benign growth.
On post-contrast scans, a benign tumor often shows mild to moderate, uniform enhancement.
The photo reveals a lack of significant surrounding swelling (edema) or inflammatory changes.
The lesion shown in the image appears solitary and does not show signs of spreading to other areas.
A key feature of this mass is its regular and predictable shape, lacking aggressive characteristics.
malignant tumor: The image displays a malignant tumor characterized by its irregular shape and indistinct, poorly defined borders.
A key feature in the photo is the infiltrative nature of the mass, which invades and destroys adjacent normal tissue.
This lesion shows a heterogeneous internal structure, with mixed signals indicating areas of necrosis or hemorrhage.
In the scan, the margins of the mass are spiculated or fuzzy, blending into the surrounding tissue.
A malignant tumor is visible, causing significant swelling (edema) in the tissues around it.
After contrast administration, the photo shows intense, irregular, or ring-like enhancement, indicating abnormal blood vessel growth.
The image reveals an aggressive growth pattern that does not respect normal anatomical boundaries.
This is a photo of a mass with a chaotic and disorganized internal appearance.
The scan shows evidence of invasion into nearby blood vessels or lymphatic channels, a sign of malignancy.
Secondary signs such as smaller satellite lesions or spread to regional lymph nodes may be visible in the image.
normal scan: The image displays normal tissue architecture, with no evidence of abnormal masses, growths, or lesions.
In this photo, the organs and structures shown are of a normal size, shape, and position.
A normal scan is characterized by the absence of any focal distortions or structural abnormalities.
The scan reveals homogeneous and expected tissue texture and signal intensity throughout the imaged area.
There are no signs of unusual fluid collections, inflammation, or swelling in this image.
This is a photo of a normal scan where all anatomical structures appear symmetrical and well-defined.
No pathological or abnormal enhancement is visible after the administration of a contrast agent.
The image shows clear and distinct boundaries between different types of tissues and organs.
The overall appearance in the photo is unremarkable and consistent with a healthy, normal study.
This scan is free of any suspicious findings that would suggest the presence of a disease process.
